clean_story:
Sun’s uneven path. Clocks that lie.

"Latitude bends time" describes how your distance from the equator stretches or shrinks daylight, warping how we measure hours. The closer you live to the poles, the more the sun behaves like a lazy pendulum—swinging wildly between extreme highs and lows instead of a steady arc.

Here’s the mechanism: Earth tilts 23.4 degrees, so sunlight hits different latitudes at skewed angles. At the equator, the sun rises and sets like a metronome: 12 hours of day, 12 of night, every day. But in Reykjavík (64 degrees north), June 21st brings 21 hours of daylight; the sun dips but never fully sets, tracing a shallow loop along the horizon like a coin rolling in slow motion. Six months later, it barely climbs above the rooftops for 4 hours. Clocks still tick 24-hour days, but the sun’s schedule is unrecognizable.

Tools expose the bend: A sundial in Nairobi and one in Oslo, set simultaneously, will disagree by hours. Modern time zones—vertical slices of the globe—force Oslo to sync with Berlin, not its own sunlight. Yet bodies resist: In polar regions, sleep patterns fracture without darkness; some mines use blackout curtains to simulate night.

Three related subjects are earth’s wobbling axis, circadian rhythms in extremes, and how calendars fail seasons.
